56: A direct revelation: 'Behold, I see the heavens opened, and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God.' Stephen's vision at the moment of martyrdom is the only post-resurrection appearance of the risen Christ to someone other than Paul in Acts (the Damascus road, Acts 9:4-5). The 'Son of Man' title — Jesus's own self-designation during his ministry (Luke 9:22; Acts 22:69) — is used here by Stephen, the only NT speaker outside the Gospels to use this title of Jesus. The standing Christ (see mkt-original note on hestōta) is the risen Lord actively receiving his first martyr. Christology is here embedded in pneumatology: the Spirit-filled Stephen sees the Christological reality that sustains martyrdom.
1: The high priest's question — 'Are these things so?' — opens the judicial hearing that becomes Stephen's great Christological sermon. The format (defendant given opportunity to speak before the Sanhedrin) mirrors Jesus's own trial (Luke 22:66-71). Stephen's response will not be a defense of himself but an indictment of the court — the pattern of the prophets who spoke truth before power, the pattern Christ himself modeled (Luke 23:2-3, 8-9).
2: 'The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham' (ho theos tēs doxēs ōphthē tō patri hēmōn Abraam) — Stephen's opening is a Christological thesis in miniature. The God who reveals himself as 'glory' (the Shekinah tradition) appeared to Abraham outside the land and before the covenant institutions. This establishes the pattern Stephen will trace: divine initiative precedes and exceeds every institutional form. The 'God of glory' will appear again at the end of the speech — in the face of the crucified Jesus, whom Stephen sees enthroned (Acts 7:55-56).
3: God's call to Abraham — 'Go out from your land and from your kindred' (Genesis 12:1) — is the paradigmatic form of the divine call that requires leaving the familiar to follow God into the unknown. The Christological echo: Jesus himself calls disciples to leave family and possessions (Luke 14:26-27); the cross is the ultimate 'going out from' the security of this age. Abraham's obedient departure without knowing where he was going (Hebrews 11:8) is the type of faith the crucified-and-risen Christ calls his followers to.
4: Abraham's journey from Ur to Haran to Canaan — a life of pilgrimage without permanent settlement — prefigures the Christological community that 'has no lasting city but seeks the city to come' (Hebrews 13:14). The Christological reading of the patriarchal narrative: the people of God are constitutively a people in movement, oriented toward promise rather than possession. Jesus himself 'had nowhere to lay his head' (Luke 9:58) — the pattern of homeless pilgrimage is the Christological mode of existence.
5: 'Yet he gave him no inheritance in it, not even a foot's length, but promised to give it to him as a possession and to his offspring after him, though he had no child' — the promise without present possession is the structure of Abrahamic faith. The Christological fulfillment: the inheritance promised to Abraham's offspring is ultimately secured through Christ, the singular seed (Galatians 3:16). Abraham believed a resurrection-like promise — a dead womb brought to life — pointing toward the resurrection of the one in whom all nations are blessed.
6: 'His offspring would be sojourners in a land belonging to others, enslaved and afflicted for four hundred years' — the Egypt prophecy (Genesis 15:13) establishes the pattern: God's people suffer in foreign servitude before the great deliverance. The Christological typology: Christ's own suffering and death is the supreme instance of the righteous one's affliction — and his resurrection is the supreme Exodus. The Exodus story that Stephen is tracing is the type whose antitype is the cross and resurrection.
7: 'But I will judge the nation that they serve, and after that they shall come out and worship me in this place' — the Exodus promise includes both divine judgment on oppressors and the gathering of God's people for worship. The Christological fulfillment: the judgment on the powers that crucified Christ is executed in the resurrection (which vindicates Christ and condemns the accusers); the gathering for worship is the apostolic community of Acts. 'This place' is ultimately not a geographic location but the presence of the risen Lord.
8: 'And he gave him the covenant of circumcision' — the sign-covenant given to Abraham and transmitted through Isaac, Jacob, and the twelve patriarchs establishes the covenant people's identity. The Christological frame: Paul will argue (Rom 4; Gal 3) that circumcision was a sign of the righteousness of faith that Abraham had before circumcision — the covenant of promise precedes and exceeds the covenant of signs. Christ fulfills the promise, not merely the sign.
9: 'And the patriarchs, jealous of Joseph, sold him into Egypt; but God was with him' (ho de theos ēn met' autou) — the Joseph narrative introduces the defining pattern of Stephen's Christological typology: the rejected deliverer whom God vindicates. Joseph is sold by his brothers (type of Christ betrayed by one of his own), suffers in Egypt (type of Christ's humiliation), but God's presence persists through the rejection and through it accomplishes the salvation of those who rejected him.
10: 'And rescued him out of all his afflictions and gave him favor and wisdom before Pharaoh, king of Egypt, who made him ruler over Egypt and over all his household' — God's vindication of Joseph through elevation to Pharaoh's right hand is the Joseph-type's closest correspondence to the resurrection-exaltation of Christ. The rejected one becomes the sovereign; the one whom brothers wanted dead becomes the one through whom they live. Christ seated at the right hand of God (Acts 2:33-34) completes what Joseph's story prefigured.
11: The famine that drove Jacob's sons to Egypt — forcing the very men who sold Joseph to come to him for grain — enacts the reversal pattern of Christological typology. The rejected one becomes the only source of life; those who rejected him must come to him or die. The gospel's logic is structurally identical: the one rejected and crucified is now the only source of life for those who crucified him (Acts 3:14-15 — 'you killed the Author of life, whom God raised').
12: 'Jacob heard that there was grain in Egypt, and he sent our fathers out on their first visit' — the first visit without recognition parallels the pattern of rejection-then-recognition that runs through both the Joseph story and Luke's Gospel (the disciples on the Emmaus road who did not recognize the risen Christ until the breaking of bread, Luke 24:13-35). Divine provision is present before recognition dawns.
13: 'On the second visit Joseph made himself known to his brothers' — the second-visit revelation (Genesis 45:1-15) is the moment of recognition and reconciliation. The Christological echo: Christ's second appearing — the parousia — will be the universal moment of recognition, when 'every eye will see him, even those who pierced him' (Revelation 1:7; cf. Zechariah 12:10). The Joseph narrative's two-visit structure prefigures the two-advent pattern of Christological revelation.
14: Joseph sent for Jacob and all his family — seventy-five souls (LXX count) — to come to Egypt. The gathering of the whole family around the vindicated Joseph corresponds to the gathering of Israel around the vindicated Christ. The Christological community is assembled by the exalted one who sends his Spirit to draw his people to himself (John 12:32 — 'when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself').
15: Jacob's death in Egypt and the burial promise — both Jacob and Joseph died there, though the promise of the land remained unfulfilled — maintains the tension of Christological promise-without-possession that Stephen is tracing. The patriarchs lived and died as sojourners whose hope reached beyond their lifetimes. The Christological community lives in the same structure: the promise is certain but its fullness awaits the parousia.
16: The burial of the patriarchs at Shechem (in the tomb Abraham bought from the sons of Hamor) — Stephen's account differs from the Genesis record (which places Jacob at Machpelah) and may reflect alternative traditions or deliberate summary. The theological point remains: the patriarchs were buried in the promised land as a down-payment on the inheritance. Their tombs are Christological pledges: God's promises outlast death, just as Christ's resurrection is God's pledge of the general resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:20-23).
17: 'As the time of the promise drew near' (kathōs de ēngizen ho chronos tēs epangelias) — the language of approach and fulfillment. The Christological reading of Exodus: Israel's multiplication and oppression are not random historical events but the eschatological intensification before God acts decisively. The pattern — suffering preceding redemption, darkness before dawn — is the template for the Christological passion-before-resurrection structure. Every 'darkest before dawn' moment in Israel's history anticipates the supreme darkness of the cross before the dawn of resurrection.
18: 'Until there arose over Egypt another king who did not know Joseph' — the erasure of Joseph from Pharaoh's memory enacts the perennial pattern: institutional power forgets its indebtedness to the one who saved it and turns against the next deliverer. The Christological echo: the chief priests who should have recognized the Messiah had forgotten the prophets' testimony to him, or suppressed it. Every generation's rejection of God's deliverer follows the same pattern of willful ignorance.
19: Pharaoh's genocidal program against Hebrew male infants — 'so that they might not be kept alive' (tou mē zōogoneisthai) — is the demonic attempt to eliminate the deliverer before he arrives. The Christological parallel: Herod's massacre of the Bethlehem infants (Matthew 2:16-18) is the typological repetition — the powers that fear the true King attempt to eliminate him at birth. The pattern of satanic preemptive violence against God's deliverer runs from Pharaoh through Herod to the cross.
20: 'At this time Moses was born; and he was beautiful in God's sight' (asteios tō theō, beautiful/acceptable to God) — the divine commendation of Moses at birth mirrors the Christological affirmation at Jesus's baptism ('You are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased', Luke 3:22). Both the type and the antitype bear God's prior approval before their public ministry begins. Moses's three-month concealment by his parents (Hebrews 11:23 — by faith) parallels the protection of the infant Jesus in Egypt.
21: The abandonment and adoption of Moses — thrown into the Nile, drawn out by Pharaoh's daughter — enacts the pattern of apparent abandonment and unexpected rescue. The Christological echo of the cross: the cry of dereliction ('my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?', Luke 23:34 implied) is the deepest expression of apparent abandonment; the resurrection is the unexpected rescue by the Father. What looked like the end became the beginning of the greater life.
22: 'Moses was instructed in all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and he was mighty in his words and deeds' — Moses's education in the highest learning of his age, combined with divine power, equipped him for his deliverer role. The Christological comparison: Jesus grew 'in wisdom and in stature and in favor with God and man' (Luke 2:52); his Nazareth teaching amazed those who heard him (Luke 4:22). Both Moses and Christ are the most fully formed human beings of their generation, equipped beyond their contemporaries for divine mission.
23: 'When he was forty years old, it came into his heart to visit his brothers, the children of Israel' — Moses's spontaneous identification with his enslaved people, though raised in privilege, is the type of the incarnation. Christ 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor' (2 Corinthians 8:9); 'he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant' (Philippians 2:6-7). Moses's voluntary identification with the afflicted foreshadows the incarnation's voluntary solidarity with the human condition.
24: Moses defended the oppressed Israelite and struck the Egyptian — the first messianic act, violent and unauthorized. The Christological contrast: Christ's defense of the oppressed was achieved not through violence against oppressors but through his own suffering. Moses's way became the type that Christ reversed: instead of the deliverer striking the oppressor, the deliverer absorbed the oppressor's violence to break its power. The contrast illuminates the newness of Christ's messianic method.
25: 'He supposed that his brothers would understand that God was giving them salvation by his hand, but they did not understand' (ou synekan) — the failure to understand the deliverer in the moment of deliverance is Stephen's first indictment. The Christological application is unmistakable: Jesus's own people 'did not know the time of their visitation' (Luke 19:44). The pattern of missed recognition — the deliverer present but unrecognized — runs from Moses to Christ. Israel's history is a history of not understanding God's chosen saviors.
26: Moses's attempt to reconcile two quarreling Israelites — 'men, you are brothers; why are you wronging each other?' — is met with immediate hostility: 'who made you a ruler and a judge over us?' The Christological parallel: Jesus the peacemaker was consistently rejected by those whose peace he sought (Luke 19:41-42 — 'would that you, even you, had known the things that make for peace'). The deliverer who comes to reconcile is accused of power-seeking by those who resist reconciliation.
27: 'Who made you a ruler and a judge over us?' — the question posed to Moses is the question posed to Jesus throughout his ministry: by what authority? (Luke 20:2). The resistance to divinely appointed authority is Stephen's core accusation against Israel's religious leaders: they have consistently asked God's messengers, 'who authorized you?' rather than recognizing the divine authorization in the messenger's works and words. The same question will be implicitly posed to Stephen himself — by the very court that poses it.
28: 'Do you want to kill me as you killed the Egyptian yesterday?' — the threat against Moses by his own kinsman forces his flight. The Christological irony sharpens: Moses, who killed an Egyptian oppressor in defense of his people, is threatened with death by the people he came to save. Jesus, who raised the dead and healed the sick for his people, was threatened and finally killed by the people he came to save (John 1:11 — 'he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him').
29: Moses's flight to Midian and forty-year sojourn — exiled from the people he came to save — corresponds to the period of Israel's rejection. The Christological typology: between Moses's first and second coming to Israel (Exodus 2-3 — the first, rejected; Exodus 3-4 — the second, accepted), there is a gap of preparation and divine equipping. Between Christ's first coming (rejected) and second coming (accepted), there is the present age of witness. The exile of the deliverer is not defeat but preparation.
30: The angel in the burning bush — 'after forty years had passed, an angel appeared to him in the wilderness of Mount Sinai, in a flame of fire in a bush' — is the divine theophany that commissions Moses for his second visit. The Christological resonance: the flame of fire that appeared in the burning bush appears again at Pentecost (Acts 2:3 — 'tongues as of fire'). The same divine presence that equipped Moses for the first Exodus equips the church for the new Exodus. The theophany is Christologically continuous.
31: Moses's amazement at the burning bush — 'as he drew near to look, there came the voice of the Lord' — combines vision and audition in the encounter with the divine. The Christological pattern: divine initiative (the fire), human response (drawing near), divine address (the voice). The structure is repeated in every conversion in Acts: the divine initiative (the Spirit's work), the human response (turning), the personal address (the gospel). Moses's burning-bush experience is the paradigm of encounter with the living God through Christ.
32: 'I am the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham and of Isaac and of Jacob.' The divine self-identification at the burning bush (Exodus 3:6) is the basis for Jesus's resurrection argument in the Gospels (Luke 20:37-38 — 'Moses showed that the dead are raised… for he calls the Lord the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob. Now he is not God of the dead, but of the living'). The burning-bush theophany, rightly read, is a resurrection proof: the God of the patriarchs is still their God, which requires that they live.
33: 'Take off the sandals from your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy ground' — the holy ground of the divine presence reveals a Christological principle: wherever God is present, the ordinary becomes sacred. The temple was 'holy ground' institutionally; but Christ declared that he would make his dwelling in any two or three gathered in his name (Matthew 18:20), and that the Spirit-indwelt body is the temple (1 Corinthians 6:19). The holy ground moves from a fixed location to wherever Christ's Spirit is present.
34: 'I have surely seen the affliction of my people who are in Egypt, and have heard their groaning, and I have come down to deliver them' (Exodus 3:7-8) — the divine sight-hearing-coming-delivering sequence is the structure of the incarnation. The Christological reading of Exod 3: what God declares to Moses is literally enacted in Christ. God sees human affliction → sends his Son; hears human groaning → the incarnate Son weeps and intercedes; comes down → the Word becomes flesh; delivers → the cross and resurrection accomplish the ultimate Exodus. Moses's commission announces what Christ accomplishes.
35: 'This Moses, whom they rejected, saying, 'Who made you a ruler and a judge?' — this man God sent as both ruler and deliverer by the hand of the angel who appeared to him in the bush.' Stephen's explicit typological statement: the rejected Moses is the one God vindicates and sends as deliverer. The Christological application is structural: this is exactly the pattern of Jesus. The double designation 'ruler and deliverer' (archonta kai lytrōtēn) corresponds to 'Lord and Christ' (Acts 2:36) — the rejected one receives from God the authority his rejectors denied him.
36: 'This man led them out, performing wonders and signs in Egypt and at the Red Sea and in the wilderness for forty years' — Moses's miracle-signs are the pattern for apostolic signs throughout Acts (Acts 2:43; Acts 4:30; Acts 5:12; Acts 6:8; Acts 8:13). Every apostolic miracle is performed in Christ's name (Acts 3:6; Acts 4:10) and derives from Christ's resurrection power. The Exodus signs are the type; the gospel's signs are the antitype. Christ is the greater Moses who performs the greater signs: raising the dead, healing at a word, casting out demons by his authority.
37: A fulfillment: 'This is the Moses who said to the Israelites, 'God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your brothers.'' (Deuteronomy 18:15) — the second time this text appears in Acts (first in 3:22 by Peter). Stephen cites it as Moses's own prophecy that transcends Moses: the deliverer Moses was would be succeeded by a greater deliverer. The Christological identification of Jesus as the Prophet-like-Moses is the hinge of Stephen's entire speech — the argument that what Moses announced, Jesus fulfills, making rejection of Jesus a rejection of Moses.
38: 'This is the one who was in the congregation in the wilderness with the angel who spoke to him at Mount Sinai, and with our fathers. He received living oracles (logia zōnta) to give to us.' Moses received the Torah as 'living oracles'; Christ gives the Spirit, who is the fulfillment of the Torah's life-giving intention (Romans 8:4 — 'the righteous requirement of the law is fulfilled in us, who walk not according to the flesh but according to the Spirit'). The living oracles of Sinai become the living water of the Spirit that Christ pours out (John 7:38-39).
39: 'Our fathers refused to obey him, but thrust him aside, and in their hearts turned to Egypt' — the wilderness generation's rejection of Moses is the definitive instance of the pattern Stephen traces. 'In their hearts they turned to Egypt' captures the perennial temptation: to return to the familiar bondage rather than follow the deliverer into the promised unknown. The Christological application: the Sanhedrin's rejection of Jesus is the contemporary instance of the same heart-Egypt. The form changes; the pattern is identical.
40: 'Saying to Aaron, 'Make for us gods who will go before us. As for this Moses who led us out from the land of Egypt, we do not know what has happened to him'' — the golden calf demand during Moses's Sinai absence is the founding instance of Israel's idolatry. The specific catalyst is the perceived absence of the leader — 'we do not know what happened to him'. The Christological echo: in the absence of the risen Christ (physically ascended), the temptation is to manufacture visible substitutes for the invisible Lord. Idolatry is always a response to perceived divine absence.
41: 'And they made a calf in those days, and offered a sacrifice to the idol and were rejoicing in the works of their hands' — the golden calf combines false worship, dead idols, and self-congratulation. The Christological contrast: true worship is directed to the risen Christ whom 'no one has ever seen' but who is present through the Spirit; true sacrifice is the 'living sacrifice' of one's whole life (Romans 12:1), not the manipulation of human craftsmanship. The 'works of their hands' (tois ergois tōn cheirōn autōn) will reappear in Acts 7:48 as the critique of the temple itself.
42: 'But God turned away and gave them over to worship the host of heaven, as it is written in the book of the prophets' — the divine 'giving over' (Amos 5:25-27 cited in Acts 7:42-43) is Paul's theological framework in Romans 1:24-28: God gives over those who exchange the truth of God for a lie. The Christological implication: the rejection of the true Deliverer consistently results in divine abandonment to lesser substitutes. The prophetic critique of Israel's idolatry that Stephen cites is simultaneously an implicit critique of the present court's rejection of Jesus.
43: The Amos citation — 'you took up the tent of Moloch and the star of your god Rephan' — identifies Israel's wilderness idolatry with the surrounding Canaanite and Babylonian cults. The Christological critique: idolatry is always syncretism — borrowing the world's worship objects to fill the void left by rejecting the living God. The tent of Moloch (a pagan portable shrine) is the perverse counterpart to the tabernacle of God's design (Acts 7:44). Israel simultaneously had the true portable sanctuary and imported the false one.
44: 'Our fathers had the tent of witness in the wilderness, just as he who spoke to Moses directed him to make it, according to the pattern that he had seen' — the tabernacle's design came from the heavenly pattern (typon, 'type') that Moses saw on Sinai (Exodus 25:9, 40). The Christological use of 'type' in Hebrews (Hebrews 8:5; Acts 9:24): the tabernacle was a copy and shadow of the heavenly sanctuary that Christ entered as high priest. The earthly tent pointed beyond itself to the true dwelling of God that Christ opened.
45: 'Our fathers in turn brought it in with Joshua when they dispossessed the nations that God drove out before our fathers. So it was until the days of David' — the tabernacle's journey from Sinai to Canaan with Joshua (whose name is the same as Jesus — both are Iēsous/Yehoshua) to David prefigures the Christological completion of the Exodus: Joshua/Jesus bringing the people of God into their inheritance. The typological connection between Joshua and Jesus (same name, same role of bringing God's people into rest) is the basis of Hebrews 4:8's argument.
46: 'David found favor in the sight of God and asked to find a dwelling place for the God of Jacob' — David's desire to build a house for God (2 Samuel 7:1-3) was answered with the Davidic covenant promise that God would build David a house (a dynastic line). The Christological fulfillment: what David wanted to do for God (build a permanent dwelling) is what God accomplished in Christ — the Word became flesh and 'tabernacled among us' (John 1:14, eskenōsen en hēmin). God solved the dwelling-place question by becoming the dwelling himself.
47: 'But it was Solomon who built a house for him' — Solomon built the temple that David wanted to build. The temple is the institutional culmination of the tabernacle trajectory. Stephen's citation without celebration: he is setting up the critique that follows. The Christological reading of the temple-building: the building is penultimate, never ultimate. It housed the divine presence provisionally; Christ is the permanent dwelling of the divine fullness (Colossians 2:9 — 'in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily').
48: A theme: 'Yet the Most High does not dwell in houses made by hands (cheiropoiētois)' — cheiropoiētos (made by human hands) is the LXX vocabulary for idols (Leviticus 26:1; Isaiah 2:18; Acts 10:11; Acts 16:12). By applying the idol-vocabulary to the temple, Stephen makes his most provocative claim: the temple, when treated as God's permanent residence, becomes functionally idolatrous — an attempt to confine the divine presence in human construction. The Christological resolution: Christ's resurrection body is the true temple (John 2:19-21 — 'destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up').
49: 'Heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool. What kind of house will you build for me, says the Lord, or what is the place of my rest?' (Isaiah 66:1-2) — the Isaiah citation makes the theological point with divine authority. A God whose throne is heaven and whose footstool is earth cannot be contained in any human building. The Christological implication: what Solomon's temple could not provide — God's permanent dwelling with humanity — Christ accomplishes: 'Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man' (Revelation 21:3). Not the temple but the incarnate, crucified, risen, glorified Son is where God truly dwells.
50: 'Did not my hand make all these things?' — the Creator's rhetorical question (Isaiah 66:2) exposes the category error of the temple ideology: the hands that made the universe cannot be housed in what human hands make. The Christological reversal: instead of human hands building a house for God, God's hands (through the Spirit, Luke 1:35 — the incarnation as divine act) built the true house — a human body. The direction of construction is reversed: not human hands housing God, but divine action creating the embodied Son.
51: 'You stiff-necked people, uncircumcised in heart and ears, you always resist the Holy Spirit. As your fathers did, so do you.' — the speech's turn: from history to indictment. Stephen's accusation has two parts: (1) they are 'uncircumcised in heart' — the Christological critique of mere external religion (Jeremiah 4:4; Deuteronomy 10:16; Romans 2:29 — 'a Jew is one inwardly, and circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit'). (2) They 'always resist the Holy Spirit' — the pattern the whole speech traced is now named. Every generation's rejection of God's deliverers is resistance to the Spirit who sent them.
52: A direct revelation: 'Which of the prophets did your fathers not persecute? And they killed those who announced beforehand the coming of the Righteous One (tou dikaiou), and you have now become his betrayers and murderers.' The 'Righteous One' (ho dikaios) is the most archaic Christological title in Acts — also used in 3:14 and Acts 22:14. It identifies Jesus with the persecuted righteous one of the Psalms (Psalms 34:19; Acts 37:12), the suffering servant of Isaiah 53:11, and the righteous sufferer of Wisdom of Solomon Acts 2:12-20. The court that is trying Stephen is the court that murdered the Righteous One.
53: 'You who received the law as delivered by angels and did not keep it' — the closing indictment connects law-reception with law-violation. The irony is judicial: the court that is prosecuting Stephen for blasphemy against the law has itself broken the law's deepest commandment (loving God with heart and soul and mind), rejected the prophets the law commended, and murdered the one all the law pointed toward. The Christological indictment: the law given through angels, if rightly kept, leads to the Christ it anticipates. To reject Christ is to reject the law's own telos.
54: 'Now when they heard these things they were enraged, and they ground their teeth at him' (dieprionto tais kardiais autōn, they were cut to their hearts in rage) — the contrast with Pentecost: there, the crowd 'were cut to the heart' (katenygesan tēn kardian) in conviction and repentance (Acts 2:37). Here, the Sanhedrin is cut to the heart in murderous rage. The same Christological proclamation produces opposite responses depending on the hearer's posture: those who are open receive conviction unto repentance; those who are closed receive condemnation unto hardening.
55: 'But he, full of the Holy Spirit, gazed into heaven and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing at the right hand of God' — the Spirit-filling enables the vision of Christological reality that sustains martyrdom. Stephen sees what Paul will confess as the content of faith: the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6). The glory (doxan tou theou) that Moses saw at Sinai, that filled Solomon's temple, is now concentrated in the face of the risen Jesus who stands to receive his martyr.
57: 'But they cried out with a loud voice and stopped their ears and rushed together at him' — the deliberate, violent refusal to hear is the bodily enactment of the 'uncircumcised ears' Stephen accused them of (Acts 7:51). They physically stop their ears to prevent the possibility that the Christological witness might penetrate. The Christological echo: 'He who has ears to hear, let him hear' (Luke 8:8) is met by those who choose not to have ears. Hearing requires willingness; rejection is always an active, not a passive, choice.
58: 'They cast him out of the city and stoned him. And the witnesses laid down their garments at the feet of a young man named Saul' — the execution pattern mirrors the execution of Jesus: led out of the city (Jesus crucified outside Jerusalem, Hebrews 13:12), killed by the establishment's authorization. Saul's first appearance is as the guardian of the executioners' garments — a passive consent that Acts will develop into active persecution (Acts 8:3). The Christological community produces its first martyr; its future chief apostle watches his death.
59: 'And as they were stoning Stephen, he called out, 'Lord Jesus, receive my spirit'' — the prayer to 'Lord Jesus' to receive his spirit is the equivalent of Jesus's own prayer to the Father at death ('Father, into your hands I commit my spirit', Luke 23:46). Where Jesus prays to the Father, Stephen prays to Jesus — a Christological equation that places Jesus in the position of the Father as the receiver of the departing spirit. The martyrdom prayer is an implicit confession of Christ's divinity: only God receives the returning spirit.
60: 'And he knelt down and cried out with a loud voice, 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them.' And when he had said this, he fell asleep.' The prayer for his killers' forgiveness (echoing Jesus's own prayer, Luke 23:34 — 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do') is the Christological mark of Stephen's martyrdom. The one who dies in Christ's pattern prays in Christ's pattern. The 'falling asleep' (ekoimēthē) vocabulary — the earliest Christian euphemism for death — reflects the resurrection faith that death for the believer is now sleep awaiting awakening (1 Corinthians 15:20; 1 Thessalonians 4:13-14). Stephen falls asleep trusting in the one who is the resurrection and the life.
